subtopics.subtopic_heading: Summary, Indictable, and Hybrid Offences — Classification
common_misconceptions: Students forget that hybrid offences are treated as indictable until the Crown elects
Students confuse s. 469 (exclusive SCJ) with s. 553 (absolute jurisdiction provincial)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Three categories: summary (minor, tried in provincial court), indictable (serious, various election options), hybrid (Crown elects, treated as indictable until election). Test: identify the category from the offence described and then determine where it can be tried.

common_misconceptions: Students think murder can be tried in provincial court
Students confuse absolute jurisdiction (s. 553, no election by accused) with exclusive jurisdiction (s. 469, SCJ only)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 469: exclusive SCJ jurisdiction (murder, treason, etc.) — no election. Section 553: absolute provincial court jurisdiction (theft under $5,000, etc.) — no election. Section 554: remaining indictable offences — accused elects. Exam trap: accused charged with robbery — can they elect provincial court?

chapters.chapter_title: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers
common_misconceptions: Students think a peace officer can arrest for any indictable offence without warrant
Students forget s. 495(2) imposes a duty NOT to arrest if public interest can be served by appearance notice
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 495(1): peace officer may arrest without warrant for indictable offence (any), hybrid (treated as indictable), or found committing any offence. Section 495(2): SHALL NOT arrest (for certain offences) if public interest can be met by compelling appearance by other means. Exam trap: officer has grounds but also has time to get a warrant — must they?

common_misconceptions: Students think citizen's arrest applies to any criminal act
Students forget the citizen arrest must be for an indictable offence committed or being committed
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 494(1): anyone may arrest without warrant a person found committing an indictable offence. Section 494(2): property owner may also arrest where offence is committed on their property. Duty to deliver arrested person to a peace officer without delay. PR angle: lawyer who advises a client on whether to conduct a citizen's arrest.

topics.topic_heading: Detention and Right to Counsel — Charter ss. 9, 10

subtopics.primary_statute: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982
common_misconceptions: Students confuse arbitrary detention with all detention
Students forget that a lawful investigative detention is still a detention but may not be arbitrary
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Arbitrary detention: detention not authorized by law, or authorized but arbitrary in its application (no reasonable grounds). Investigative detention (Mann test): reasonable grounds to suspect (not believe) the person is connected to a crime, and the detention is reasonably necessary. Test: is a 'stop-and-identify' demand a detention?

subtopics.subtopic_heading: s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel
subtopics.primary_statute: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982
common_misconceptions: Students think the right to counsel arises only on formal arrest
Students forget the implementational duties (facilitating access, ceasing questioning)
Students confuse informational duty with implementational duty
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Two duties on police: (1) informational — inform of right to retain and instruct counsel without delay, (2) implementational — provide reasonable opportunity to exercise the right, cease questioning/taking samples until waiver or opportunity given. Exam trap: accused says 'I don't need a lawyer' — is that a valid waiver? Must be informed and voluntary.

subtopics.primary_statute: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982
common_misconceptions: Students think all warrantless searches are automatically unreasonable
Students forget the reasonable expectation of privacy is the threshold question
Students confuse the grounds for a warrant (reasonable grounds to believe) with investigative detention (reasonable grounds to suspect)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 8 protects against unreasonable search and seizure. Requires: (1) reasonable expectation of privacy, (2) search must be authorized by law, (3) law authorizing search must itself be reasonable, (4) search must be conducted reasonably. Warrantless searches are presumptively unreasonable. Exceptions: search incident to arrest, consent, exigent circumstances.

common_misconceptions: Students think police can search anywhere authorized by the warrant
Students forget the informant must appear before a justice in person (telewarrants are a separate provision)
Students confuse ITO (information to obtain) requirements
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 487(1): justice may issue search warrant on sworn information showing reasonable grounds to believe: offence committed, evidence will be found at the place. ITO must contain sufficient grounds — bald assertions insufficient. Test: ITO with stale information — does it still support the warrant?

common_misconceptions: Students think police can search anywhere incident to arrest
Students forget the search must be truly incident to the arrest — connected in purpose
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Common law power: upon valid arrest, police may search the person and their immediate surroundings for weapons, evidence, or escape tools. Must be truly incident (temporal and causal connection). Exam trap: arrest made outside house, then police search the house — is this incident to arrest? Generally no.

common_misconceptions: Students confuse the three grounds and describe them imprecisely
Students forget that the tertiary ground (public confidence) requires a balancing test
Students think detention under the tertiary ground is routine
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Primary: ensure court attendance. Secondary: protection of the public (substantial likelihood of committing further offence or interfering with witnesses). Tertiary: maintain public confidence in justice system (not routine — reserved for serious offences where release would shake public confidence). Exam trap: Crown relies on tertiary ground for a minor offence — is it available?

common_misconceptions: Students think reverse onus applies to all serious offences
Students forget the specific offences listed in s. 515(6)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 515(6): the accused bears the burden of showing cause for release (not the Crown) for specific offences including: charged while on another release, drug trafficking, terrorism, firearm offences. Test: identify whether the charge triggers reverse onus.

common_misconceptions: Students think the court can impose any conditions it deems appropriate
Students forget the ladder principle — least restrictive first
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Ladder principle: the justice must start at the least restrictive option and only escalate if necessary. Order of escalation: (1) release on undertaking with no conditions, (2) with conditions, (3) with surety. PR angle: lawyer who agrees to onerous conditions without negotiating less restrictive alternatives.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles
common_misconceptions: Students think the Crown must disclose only what it plans to use at trial
Students forget disclosure is a constitutional obligation (ss. 7, 11(b) Charter)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Stinchcombe: Crown must disclose all information in its possession that is relevant, whether it intends to use it or not, and whether it is inculpatory or exculpatory. Two exceptions: clearly irrelevant, and privilege. Disclosure is an ongoing obligation. PR angle: Crown counsel who fails to disclose exculpatory evidence.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Third Party Records and Privacy (O'Connor, Mills)
common_misconceptions: Students apply O'Connor procedure to all third party records
Students forget that ss. 278.1-278.91 govern records of complainants in sexual offence proceedings
subtopics.notes_for_generation: For records relating to complainant's personal history in sexual offences: ss. 278.1-278.91 regime (more stringent — accused must show likely relevant, must not rely on rape myths). For other third party records: O'Connor procedure (judge reviews in camera, weighs competing interests). PR angle: defence lawyer who improperly seeks complainant's therapy records.

common_misconceptions: Students forget that not all indictable offences allow an election
Students confuse re-election procedures
subtopics.notes_for_generation: For non-s.469, non-s.553 indictable offences: accused may elect (1) judge alone in provincial court, (2) judge alone in SCJ, (3) judge and jury in SCJ. Re-election: accused may re-elect with consent of Crown. PR angle: lawyer who fails to properly advise on the advantages and disadvantages of each mode.

common_misconceptions: Students think any guilty plea is automatically valid
Students forget the judge must be satisfied on three conditions before accepting a guilty plea
Students forget the plea must be to a specific charge
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 606(1.1): the court shall not accept a guilty plea unless satisfied that the accused: (a) is making the plea voluntarily, (b) understands the nature of the charge, (c) understands the consequences of the plea, and (d) understands that by pleading guilty they are admitting the essential elements. PR angle: lawyer who coaches a client to plead guilty to a charge they did not commit.

common_misconceptions: Students confuse the standard of proof between civil and criminal
Students think reasonable doubt is the same as balance of probabilities
Students forget that neither guilt nor innocence need be proved — only that the Crown has not proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Charter s. 11(d): presumption of innocence until proved guilty according to law. Standard: beyond a reasonable doubt — not absolute certainty, not balance of probabilities. Crown bears the burden. Exam trap: if the jury thinks it's 60% likely the accused committed the offence, must they convict? No — reasonable doubt exists.

common_misconceptions: Students think a voir dire can only be held to determine voluntariness of a statement
Students forget the jury is absent during a voir dire
subtopics.notes_for_generation: A voir dire is a trial within a trial to determine the admissibility of evidence. Jury is excluded. Common uses: admissibility of a confession (voluntariness), admissibility of evidence obtained by Charter breach. PR angle: defence counsel who does not request a voir dire when a statement's voluntariness is in question.

subtopics.primary_statute: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982
common_misconceptions: Students think all Charter breaches result in exclusion of evidence
Students apply the wrong test — they omit the 'bring administration of justice into disrepute' analysis
Students confuse s. 24(1) (any remedy) with s. 24(2) (exclusion of evidence)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 24(1): any remedy the court considers appropriate and just (stay of proceedings, declaratory relief, damages). Section 24(2): evidence obtained in breach of Charter shall be excluded if admission would bring the administration of justice into disrepute. Grant test (2009): three factors — seriousness of the breach, impact on the accused's Charter-protected interests, society's interest in adjudication on the merits. Exam trap: minor administrative breach — automatic exclusion? No — apply Grant test.

subtopics.primary_statute: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5
common_misconceptions: Students think hearsay is any out-of-court statement
Students forget the definition requires: out of court, offered for the truth of its contents
Students confuse hearsay exceptions with principled exceptions
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Hearsay: out-of-court statement tendered to prove the truth of its contents. Principled approach (Khelawon): exceptions justified by necessity and reliability. Traditional exceptions: admissions by a party, dying declarations, prior inconsistent statements, business records. Test: is this statement being used for its truth or for another purpose (e.g., to show the statement was made)?

subtopics.primary_statute: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5
common_misconceptions: Students think a prior inconsistent statement is automatically evidence of its truth
Students confuse using the statement to impeach vs. as substantive evidence
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 9(1): a party may cross-examine their own witness on a prior inconsistent statement if they are declared adverse. Section 9(2): if the prior statement was made under oath in a judicial proceeding, it may be used as substantive evidence (not just for impeachment). Exam trap: witness at trial says something different from what they said in a police statement — what can defence counsel do?

common_misconceptions: Students think prior sexual activity between complainant and accused is always admissible
Students forget the mandatory hearing under s. 276(2) before the evidence can be admitted
Students forget the twin myths that s. 276 is designed to exclude
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 276(1): evidence of complainant's prior sexual activity is not admissible to support an inference that the complainant (a) consented, or (b) is less credible. Section 276(2): the accused must apply for a hearing; the court must determine admissibility before the evidence can be adduced. The two forbidden inferences are the 'twin myths'. PR angle: defence counsel who attempts to elicit this evidence without a s. 276 hearing.

common_misconceptions: Students confuse NCR (at the time of the act) with unfit to stand trial (at time of trial)
Students forget the NCR verdict is not an acquittal — the Review Board takes over
Students think the burden of proof on the NCR defence is on the Crown
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 16(1): not criminally responsible if, at the time of the act, suffering from a mental disorder that rendered the person incapable of: (a) appreciating the nature and quality of the act, OR (b) knowing that it was wrong. Burden: on the party raising the defence — balance of probabilities. If NCR: not an acquittal — Review Board makes a disposition (absolute discharge, conditional discharge, or custody).

common_misconceptions: Students confuse fitness (current — at time of trial) with NCR (past — at time of act)
Students think a finding of unfitness is a final determination
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Fitness: ability at the time of trial to understand the nature of the proceedings and communicate with counsel. Test can be raised at any time by any party. If unfit: stay of proceedings, treatment order to restore fitness. Distinguished from NCR: NCR looks at state of mind at the time of the offence.

common_misconceptions: Students apply the old three-part subjective test rather than the reformed s. 34
Students forget the proportionality factor
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Modern s. 34: three elements — (1) belief on reasonable grounds that force is being used or threatened, (2) act committed for the purpose of defending themselves or another, (3) act is reasonable in the circumstances. Reasonableness assessed on all circumstances (s. 34(2)). The list of factors is not exhaustive. Exam trap: excessive force — does s. 34 still apply?

common_misconceptions: Students confuse necessity (no human threat) with duress (threat from a person)
Students think s. 17 codifies all duress — but common law duress (from another person) coexists with s. 17
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Necessity (common law): imminent peril or danger, no reasonable legal alternative, proportionality between harm avoided and harm caused. Duress (s. 17 + common law): explicit threat of death or bodily harm, present and imminent, no safe avenue of escape. Section 17 is narrowly construed. Distinguish: car thief forced at gunpoint (duress) vs. driver who runs red light to get to hospital (necessity).

common_misconceptions: Students think intoxication is a full defence to any offence
Students forget the distinction between specific and general intent offences
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Intoxication is only available for specific intent offences (murder, robbery, etc.) — not general intent offences (assault, sexual assault). Section 33.1: extreme intoxication (automatism-level) is not available as a defence to general intent offences involving violence after the Neumann/Sullivan analysis. Exam trap: accused is severely intoxicated during sexual assault — can they raise intoxication?

common_misconceptions: Students list deterrence as the fundamental purpose — it is one of many objectives, not the fundamental purpose
Students forget s. 718.1 proportionality is the fundamental principle
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Fundamental purpose (s. 718): protect society and contribute to a just, peaceful, safe society. Objectives: denunciation, deterrence, separation, rehabilitation, reparations, promoting a sense of responsibility. Fundamental principle (s. 718.1): sentence must be proportionate to the gravity of the offence and the degree of responsibility. Aggravating/mitigating factors (s. 718.2). PR angle: counsel who fails to make submissions on all relevant mitigating factors.

common_misconceptions: Students forget conditional sentence orders are not available for offences with mandatory minimums
Students confuse absolute discharge (no conviction recorded) with conditional discharge (conviction recorded if conditions breached)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Options in ascending order: absolute discharge, conditional discharge, suspended sentence with probation, fine, conditional sentence order (served in community), imprisonment. Conditional sentences (s. 742.1): sentence under 2 years, judge satisfied the offender's serving in community does not endanger public safety, no mandatory minimum. Exam trap: client gets 18-month conditional sentence — is a conviction registered? Yes.

common_misconceptions: Students think victim impact statements must be considered by the sentencing judge
Students forget restitution is an order on the offender, not a civil judgment
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Victim impact statement: the court shall consider it if presented (s. 722). Restitution order (s. 738): court may order the offender to pay restitution for financial loss caused by the offence. PR angle: counsel who does not advise a victim-client about the right to make a victim impact statement.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Disputed Facts at Sentencing — Newton Hearing
common_misconceptions: Students think any disputed fact must be proven beyond a reasonable doubt at sentencing
Students confuse aggravating facts (Crown must prove BARD) with mitigating facts (accused must prove on balance of probabilities)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Section 724(3): disputed aggravating facts must be proven by the Crown beyond a reasonable doubt. Mitigating facts: proven by the offender on a balance of probabilities. A Newton hearing is held when facts are disputed. Exam trap: Crown alleges aggravating circumstances in PSR — defence disputes — what is the standard?

subtopics.primary_statute: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1
common_misconceptions: Students apply adult criminal law principles to youth matters
Students think deterrence is a primary principle under the YCJA
subtopics.notes_for_generation: The YCJA emphasizes: rehabilitation and reintegration, meaningful consequences appropriate to youth's developmental stage, reduced emphasis on deterrence compared to adult system, accountability without stigmatization. Test: a student who says youth should be deterred as adults are — is this consistent with the YCJA?

subtopics.primary_statute: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1
common_misconceptions: Students think custody is the primary option for serious youth offences
Students forget extrajudicial measures must be considered first
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Youth sentences in s. 42(2): reprimand, absolute discharge, conditional discharge, fine, community service, probation, deferred custody and supervision order, intensive support and supervision, custody and supervision. Extrajudicial measures must be considered before formal proceedings. Adult sentence: requires separate application — youth must be 14+, serious violent offence.

subtopics.primary_statute: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1
common_misconceptions: Students think adult sentences are automatically imposed for serious offences
Students forget the burden is on the Crown to justify an adult sentence
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Crown may apply for an adult sentence (accused 14+). Court must first determine whether a youth sentence would be of sufficient length to hold the young person accountable. If not, court may impose adult sentence. Crown bears the burden. Exam trap: 15-year-old charged with attempted murder — is an adult sentence automatic? No — requires application and Crown must satisfy the test.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Summary Conviction Appeal — Route and Grounds
common_misconceptions: Students think summary conviction appeals go to the Court of Appeal
Students forget the appeal from summary conviction goes to SCJ (not ONCA)
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Summary conviction appeals: from provincial court judge to SCJ. Grounds: error of law, factual findings unreasonable, miscarriage of justice. Further appeal to ONCA requires leave on a question of law alone (s. 839). Exam trap: client convicted summarily, wants to appeal — where does the appeal go?

common_misconceptions: Students think accused can appeal against conviction on any ground without leave
Students confuse accused's appeal rights with Crown's more limited rights
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Accused may appeal conviction: as of right on questions of law, with leave on mixed law and fact or fact alone. Crown may appeal acquittal: only on questions of law. Powers of Court of Appeal (s. 686(1)): allow appeal (if wrong decision on law, unreasonable verdict, or miscarriage), dismiss appeal, or apply curative proviso (s. 686(1)(b)(iii) — no substantial wrong despite error). PR angle: counsel who fails to advise on grounds for appeal.

chapters.chapter_title: DNA Data Bank Orders and Sex Offender Registry
chapters.lso_dtoc_ref: DNA Identification Act; Criminal Code ss.487.04–487.09; SOIRA ss.490.011–490.032

subtopics.subtopic_heading: DNA Data Bank Orders — Primary and Secondary Designated Offences
common_misconceptions: Students think the court always has discretion — for primary designated offences on indictment the order is mandatory unless the impact on privacy/dignity is grossly disproportionate.
Secondary designated offences require the Crown to apply and the court to be satisfied it is in the best interests of the administration of justice.
Retroactive orders (s.487.055) apply only to dangerous offenders and specific sex offenders already serving sentences.
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Focus on primary vs secondary distinction, the mandatory/discretionary split, and the grossly disproportionate exception. Exam trap: a s.487.052(2) order for secondary offences is never automatic.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Sex Offender Information Registration — SOIRA ss.490.011–490.032
common_misconceptions: Students confuse the automatic obligation (on conviction for designated sex offence) with the court's discretion to exempt.
Duration varies: 10 years (max 2 years or less), life (repeat or max 14+ years or life sentence).
Exemption requires offender to establish registration would be grossly disproportionate to public-interest purpose.
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Cover mandatory vs discretionary registration, reporting obligations, duration rules, and the exemption application. Distinguish from DNA orders. Flag that failure to comply is a separate Criminal Code offence.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Dangerous Offender Designation — s.753 Criminal Code
common_misconceptions: Students think a DO designation automatically means indeterminate detention — the court still has discretion to impose a fixed sentence plus long-term supervision if appropriate.
The Crown must apply before or at sentencing and must prove the predicate offence is a 'serious personal injury offence' as defined in s.752.
Exam trap: s.753(4.1) creates a presumption of indeterminate detention once the DO designation is made — the offender must rebut.
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Emphasise the definition of 'serious personal injury offence', the pattern-of-behaviour requirement, the Crown's burden (beyond a reasonable doubt on the predicate; balance of probabilities on the pattern), and the s.753(4.1) presumption. Distinguish DO from LTO.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Long-Term Offender Designation — s.753.1 Criminal Code
common_misconceptions: Students confuse LTO supervision (community) with DO indeterminate sentence.
LTO designation is available only where the court is not satisfied the DO criteria are met but there is a substantial risk of reoffending.
Supervision period under s.753.2 is up to 10 years and is served in the community after the custodial sentence.
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Focus on criteria distinguishing LTO from DO, the 10-year supervision maximum, and breach consequences under s.753.3. Note the court may impose LTO as an alternative to DO where appropriate.

chapters.chapter_title: Proceeds of Crime and Asset Forfeiture

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Restraint Orders and Preservation of Proceeds — s.462.33
common_misconceptions: Students think restraint requires a conviction — it is available pre-trial on application by the Attorney General.
Third parties with legitimate interest in restrained property may apply to vary the order under s.462.34.
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Cover the ex parte application basis, the required reasonable grounds, third-party variance rights, and the effect of a restraint order on dealing with property.

subtopics.subtopic_heading: Forfeiture Orders at Sentencing — ss.462.37 and 462.38
common_misconceptions: Students think forfeiture is automatic on conviction for a designated offence — the Crown must still apply and prove proceeds beyond reasonable doubt.
Fine in lieu of forfeiture under s.462.37(3) applies where property cannot be made subject to forfeiture (already dissipated).
subtopics.notes_for_generation: Distinguish mandatory forfeiture (where property is proved to be proceeds of a designated offence) from discretionary forfeiture. Cover fine-in-lieu and the notice requirement to third parties under s.462.41.
